item.title: “And what, Ānanda, is the path, the way leading to the abandoning of the five lower fetters? Here, Ānanda, a monk, by the relinquishment of…
item.description:
“And what, Ānanda, is the path, the way leading to the abandoning of the five lower fetters? Here, Ānanda, a monk, by the relinquishment of attachments (upadhiviveka), by the abandoning of unwholesome states, by the complete calming of bodily coarseness, secluded from sensual desires, secluded from unwholesome states, enters and dwells in the first jhāna, which is accompanied by initial and sustained thought, with rapture and pleasure born of seclusion.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 64 – Mahāmāluṅkyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monk, ‘ant-hill’ is a synonym for this body composed of the four great elements, born of father and mother, nourished with rice and porridge, impermanent,…
item.description:
“Monk, ‘ant-hill’ is a synonym for this body composed of the four great elements, born of father and mother, nourished with rice and porridge, impermanent, subject to rubbing, pressing, breaking, disintegration, dissolution. ‘Smoking by day and blazing by night’—what belongs to daytime work, reflected upon at night—that is ‘smoking by night.’ What, after reflecting at night, is practiced during the day by body, speech, and mind—that is ‘blazing by day.’ ‘Brahmin’ is a synonym for the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One. ‘Wise man’ is a synonym for a monk in higher training. ‘Sword’ is a synonym for noble wisdom. ‘Digging up’ is a synonym for arousing effort, striving. ‘Door-bolt’ is a synonym for ignorance; ‘lifting the door-bolt’ means abandoning that ignorance. ‘The wise man, taking the sword, dug up’ is the meaning.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 23 – Vammikasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, one who dwells seeing gratification in things that are fetters — greed is not abandoned, hatred is not abandoned, delusion is not abandoned. Because…
item.description:
“Monks, one who dwells seeing gratification in things that are fetters — greed is not abandoned, hatred is not abandoned, delusion is not abandoned. Because greed, hatred, and delusion are not abandoned, he is not freed from birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair. I say that he is not freed from suffering.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 2.6
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Few among humans Reach the further shore, While the rest of humankind Run up and down on this shore. Those who practice the Dhamma In…
item.description:
Few among humans
Reach the further shore,
While the rest of humankind
Run up and down on this shore.
Those who practice the Dhamma
In accordance with the well-taught Dhamma,
Will reach the further shore,
Beyond the realm of death, so hard to escape.
The wise, having left the dark state,
Should develop the bright,
Leaving home for the homeless life,
Living in seclusion, where pleasure is hard to find.
Seeking delight in Nibbāna,
Having abandoned sensual pleasures, without possessions,
The wise should cleanse themselves
Of defilements of the mind.
Those who have well developed the factors of enlightenment
With a mind rightly directed,
Who, without clinging, delight in the abandoning of grasping —
They, the defilement-free, the radiant,
Attain final Nibbāna in the world.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 10.117
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: All formations are impermanent, Having arisen, they must cease; Having arisen, they are subject to cessation, Their calming is bliss.
item.description:
All formations are impermanent,
Having arisen, they must cease;
Having arisen, they are subject to cessation,
Their calming is bliss.

From: Dīgha Nikāya 16 – Mahāparinibbānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “One who restrains evil intent, Seeing the beautiful with sharp desire — That person’s craving increases; He makes the fetter strong.” “One who delights in…
item.description:
“One who restrains evil intent,
Seeing the beautiful with sharp desire —
That person’s craving increases;
He makes the fetter strong.”

“One who delights in calming the mind,
Always contemplating foulness, mindful —
That person will destroy craving;
He cuts off Māra’s fetter.”

“One who has reached the goal, without fear,
Free from craving, without defilement,
Having pulled out the dart of existence —
This body is his last.”

From: Dhammapada 334-359
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Furthermore, Meghiya, for a monk well established in these five things, four more things are to be developed: foulness should be developed to abandon greed;…
item.description:
“Furthermore, Meghiya, for a monk well established in these five things, four more things are to be developed: foulness should be developed to abandon greed; loving-kindness should be developed to abandon ill-will; mindfulness of breathing should be developed to cut off thoughts; the perception of impermanence should be developed to uproot the conceit ‘I am.’ For one with the perception of impermanence, Meghiya, the perception of not-self becomes established. For one with the perception of not-self, the uprooting of the conceit ‘I am’ is attained, Nibbāna in this very life.”

From: Udana 4.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: The eye is unguarded, The faculties are unrestrained — Flies will swarm Thoughts of craving. The monk becomes rotten, Filled with foul smells — Far,…
item.description:
The eye is unguarded,
The faculties are unrestrained —
Flies will swarm
Thoughts of craving.
The monk becomes rotten,
Filled with foul smells —
Far, far away is Nibbāna!
He meets with misfortune.
In village or forest,
If one’s self is not peaceful,
The fool, without wisdom,
Walking or standing, is full of flies.
But one endowed with virtue,
Delighting in tranquil wisdom,
Lives peacefully and happily —
All flies are destroyed.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.128
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And, Migajāla, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable … enticing. If a monk does not delight in them, does not praise…
item.description:
“And, Migajāla, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable … enticing. If a monk does not delight in them, does not praise them, and dwells without lust for them, then because he does not delight, does not praise, and dwells without lust, delight ceases. With the cessation of delight, lust ceases. With the cessation of lust, bondage ceases. Not being bound by the bondage of delight, Migajāla, that monk is called ‘one who dwells alone.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.63
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Good sirs, what is the cessation of suffering? It is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, the abandoning and relinquishing of…
item.description:
“Good sirs, what is the cessation of suffering? It is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, the abandoning and relinquishing of it, freedom from it, non-reliance on it. Good sirs, what is the path leading to the cessation of suffering? It is the Noble Eightfold Path that leads to the cessation of suffering, namely: Right View, Right Intention, Right Speech, Right Action, Right Livelihood, Right Effort, Right Mindfulness, Right Concentration.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 9 – Sammādiṭṭhisutta
📖 Read this sutta
